name: Kanada
dates_notes: Dating is anchored only by the relative position of the Vaiśeṣika-Sūtra to the Nyāya-Sūtra and to the early Buddhist Abhidharma; Frauwallner places Kaṇāda in the late centuries BCE, Halbfass in the first century BCE to first century CE. The biographical figure is a name attached to a sūtra-text; nothing personal is reliably known.

lineage_polemical.note: Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.11–17 refutes the Vaiśeṣika paramāṇu-kāraṇa-vāda — the doctrine that atoms are the world-cause — through the structural impossibility of unconscious atoms initiating the first conjunction (saṃyoga) at creation.

Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 2.2.11–17 develops Bādarāyaṇa's refutation: Śaṅkara argues that paramāṇus, being acetana, cannot self-initiate conjunction; a conscious cause is required, contrary to Vaiśeṣika atheism.

Madhva is unusual among Vedāntins in redeploying Vaiśeṣika categories positively — viśeṣas are central to his ontology of internal differentiation within otherwise-identical entities (svagata-bheda); the relation is constructive on padārtha-theory, polemical on atomistic cosmology.

core_thesis: Kaṇāda's *Vaiśeṣika-Sūtra* founds a darśana of categorial realism. Reality is exhaustively analysable into six *padārthas* — *dravya* (substance), *guṇa* (quality), *karma* (motion), *sāmānya* (universal), *viśeṣa* (ultimate particular), *samavāya* (inherence) (Vaiśeṣika-Sūtra 1.1.4). The school's name derives from *viśeṣa*, the ultimate-particular category that distinguishes otherwise-indiscernible eternal substances (paradigmatically *paramāṇus*) (VS 1.2.3–6 and 7.1.8–11). The cosmology is atomistic: macroscopic substances are composed of *paramāṇus* of four element-types (earth, water, fire, air) (VS 4.1.1–7), which combine first into dyads (*dvy-aṇuka*) then into triads (*try-aṇuka*) under the impulse of *adṛṣṭa* (the unseen residue of past action) (VS 5.2.13–17 and 6.2.1–2). The four non-atomic eternal *dravyas* — *ākāśa* (VS 2.1.27–31), *kāla* (VS 2.2.6–9), *dik* (VS 2.2.10–14), *ātman* (VS 3.2.4–18) — complete the inventory. *Mokṣa* is the cessation of all nine *ātma-viśeṣa-guṇas* (cognitions, pleasures, pains, desires, aversions, volitions, *dharma*, *adharma*, *saṃskāra*) — a cool, qualityless persistence of *ātman* without consciousness as a present quality (VS 5.2.18 and 10.2.9, on the standard commentarial reading). The *Vaiśeṣika-Sūtra* in this form does not name *īśvara*; the theistic reformulation enters with Praśastapāda. The *padārtha*-grid becomes the basic vocabulary of every later Indian realism.

engaged_works.title: Vaisesika-Sutra
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composition fixed only by the text's relative position to the Nyāya-Sūtra and early Abhidharma.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Uniformly ascribed to Kaṇāda (also Kāśyapa, Ulūka) by tradition; the composite-text question — whether the surviving sūtras are a single redaction or a layered compilation — remains open in modern scholarship.

ascription_evidence.description: Frauwallner (Geschichte der indischen Philosophie II); Halbfass (1992, On Being and What There Is); Matilal (Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika).

engaged_works.summary: Ten *adhyāyas*, each in two *āhnikas*, of compressed *sūtras*. The first *adhyāya* (VS 1.1.1–4) states the six *padārthas* and gives the school's account of *dharma* as the means to *abhyudaya* (worldly flourishing) and *niḥśreyasa* (the highest good) (VS 1.1.2). The second and third *adhyāyas* (VS 2.1–3.2) analyse *dravyas* — earth, water, fire, air, *ākāśa*, *kāla*, *dik*, *ātman*, *manas* — and establish the inferences for the supersensible substances (*ātman* from desire and aversion at VS 3.2.4; *manas* from the impossibility of multiple simultaneous cognitions at VS 3.2.1–3). The fourth and fifth *adhyāyas* (VS 4.1–5.2) analyse *guṇa* and *karma*. The sixth *adhyāya* (VS 6.1–6.2) gives the school's ethics — *dharma* as Vedic *codanā*-conformity supplemented by *abhyudaya*-promoting acts. The seventh *adhyāya* (VS 7.1–7.2) analyses *sāmānya*, *viśeṣa*, and *samavāya* (esp. VS 7.2.26–31). The eighth and ninth *adhyāyas* (VS 8.1–9.2) analyse cognition and the *vidyās*, including the doctrine of *yogi-pratyakṣa* (VS 9.1.11–15). The tenth *adhyāya* (VS 10.1–10.2) analyses pleasure, pain, and the *mokṣa*-doctrine: cessation of the nine *viśeṣa-guṇas* of *ātman* (VS 10.2.9). The text is unintelligible without commentary; the principal classical commentaries are Candrānanda's *Vṛtti* and Praśastapāda's later, recasting *Padārtha-Dharma-Saṅgraha*.
